event: Pre-ceramic Archaic (Ortoiroid) peoples migrate into the islands, leaving early evidence of human habitation.
Ceramic-age Saladoid (Arawakan) groups from South America settle the islands, introducing pottery and agriculture.
Taino-related Arawak communities establish villages, cultivating cassava and living in chiefdom societies.
Carib (Kalinago) peoples expand through the Lesser Antilles, raiding and pressuring Taino settlements in the region.
Christopher Columbus sights the islands on his second voyage and names them Las Once Mil Virgenes after Saint Ursula.
Sir Francis Drake passes through the channel that now bears his name while sailing to attack Spanish Puerto Rico.
Dutch buccaneers and privateers establish a settlement on Tortola, using the islands as a base.
English planters and privateers seize Tortola from the Dutch during conflict between the two powers.
England formally annexes Tortola and the surrounding islands, attaching them to the Leeward Islands colony.
Sugar plantations expand and the importation of enslaved Africans accelerates to supply plantation labor.
Quaker settlers arrive on Tortola, later founding a community that opposed slavery.
A legislative assembly and council are established, giving the islands a measure of local government.
The first elected House of Assembly and a formal constitution and civil government are introduced.
Sugar production peaks as the plantation economy dominates the islands' export trade.
Britain abolishes the transatlantic slave trade, ending the legal importation of enslaved Africans.
An enslaved man named Arthur Hodge's earlier execution case highlights brutal plantation conditions amid abolitionist pressure.
Slavery is abolished throughout the British Empire, emancipating the enslaved population under an apprenticeship system.
Full emancipation takes effect as the apprenticeship system ends, freeing all formerly enslaved people.
Riots erupt over a cattle tax, and rebellious freedmen burn plantations, hastening the decline of the sugar economy.
A powerful hurricane and tsunami devastate the islands, worsening economic hardship and depopulation.
The British Virgin Islands are incorporated into the federal colony of the Leeward Islands.
The local legislature is abolished and the islands are administered directly as part of the Leeward Islands colony.
The United States purchases the neighboring Danish West Indies, creating the US Virgin Islands as a close neighbor.
Protesters march to Road Town demanding restoration of representative government, prompting constitutional reform.
A partly elected Legislative Council is restored following the protest movement.
The Leeward Islands colony is dissolved and the British Virgin Islands become a separate Crown colony.
The islands are placed under their own administrator, separating governance from the West Indies Federation arrangements.
A new constitution grants greater internal self-government to the territory.
A ministerial system of government is introduced, with Hamilton Lavity Stoutt becoming the first Chief Minister.
The islands choose to remain a British dependency rather than join other Caribbean states, charting a separate course.
A revised constitution expands the territory's autonomy and powers of self-government.
The International Business Companies Act is enacted, launching the territory's rise as a major offshore financial center.
Hurricane Marilyn strikes the islands, causing significant damage.
The British Overseas Territories Act grants full British citizenship to British Virgin Islanders.
A new constitution comes into force, renaming the territory's leader Premier and strengthening self-government.
Hurricane Irma devastates the islands as one of the strongest Atlantic hurricanes on record, followed by Hurricane Maria.
Premier Andrew Fahie is arrested in a US drug-smuggling sting, triggering a UK commission of inquiry into governance.
Reforms are implemented in response to the commission of inquiry while the territory avoids direct UK rule.
